raw_consumer_statement: On XX/XX/XXXX I got a call from Goodyear Tire Store XXXX XXXX XXXX, AZ. They wanted to set up a time to install new tires that were charged to my now closed Goodyear credit card. The Goodyear Credit Plan is through Citibank. I contacted XXXX XXXX of the store and explained that the charges were fraudulent and I did not order tires. I filed a fraud report with the Goodyear Credit Card for charges made on XX/XX/XXXX {$470.00}, XX/XX/XXXX {$510.00}, XX/XX/XXXX {$1100.00}. I got a response dated XX/XX/XXXX stating that Goodyear was looking into the disputed charges. I filed an identity theft report with the XXXX XXXX XXXX California XXXX Police Department regarding the unauthorized charges. I closed the Goodyear Credit Card account on XX/XX/XXXX. I got a letter delivered electronically dated XX/XX/XXXX from Goodyear indicating that they didn't have enough information to resolve the dispute and I needed to pay the charges. When I got a notice my payment was due I realized the letter was in my online account messages. On XX/XX/XXXX I called the phone number on the letter asking where I could send a letter requesting additional information. I send a letter that same day requesting the following : copies of the receipts for each charge including store number, address and phone number ; the specific tires purchased, signature of the person purchasing the tires ; copies of the service work order with information about the date and time of installing the tires ; the make, model license plate and VIN number of the vehicles on which the tires were installed and copies of their investigation documentation. I noted I planned on submitting the information to the Police Department to aid in the identity theft investigation. On XX/XX/XXXX I received a form letter stating that I am responsible for the charges on my account. None of the information I requested was provided. I still don't even know what tires were purchased.
